title: Order Execution Anomaly Detection
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="oms:order"
| transaction client_order_id maxspan=2s
| eval cancels=mvcount(if(action="cancel",1,null()))
| eval submits=mvcount(if(action="new",1,null()))
| where submits>10 AND cancels/submits>0.8
| table _time, symbol, client_order_id, submits, cancels
description: Flags unusual order patterns (layering, spoofing precursors) for market abuse surveillance.
value: Flags unusual order patterns (layering, spoofing precursors) for market abuse surveillance.
implementation: Tune for legitimate market making; integrate with surveillance team playbooks and MAR/SEC retention.
visualization: Table (suspicious clusters), Timeline (order lifecycle), Bar chart (cancel ratio by desk).
knownFalsePositives: Order-throttling and circuit-breaker traffic during volatility, auction opens, and algorithm tuning; expected under risk committee rules.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: OMS logs, exchange drop copies.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="oms:order"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Tune for legitimate market making; integrate with surveillance team playbooks and MAR/SEC retention.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="oms:order"
| transaction client_order_id maxspan=2s
| eval cancels=mvcount(if(action="cancel",1,null()))
| eval submits=mvcount(if(action="new",1,null()))
| where submits>10 AND cancels/submits>0.8
| table _time, symbol, client_order_id, submits, cancels
```

Understanding this SPL

**Order Execution Anomaly Detection** — Flags unusual order patterns (layering, spoofing precursors) for market abuse surveillance.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="oms:order"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OMS logs, exchange drop copies. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: oms:order. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="oms:order". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Groups related events into transactions — prefer `maxspan`/`maxpause`/`maxevents` for bounded memory.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **cancels** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **submits** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where submits>10 AND cancels/submits>0.8` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Order Execution Anomaly Detection**): table _time, symbol, client_order_id, submits, cancels


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (suspicious clusters), Timeline (order lifecycle), Bar chart (cancel ratio by desk).
grandmaExplanation: In Financial Services, we pay close attention to "Order Execution Anomaly Detection" in our day-to-day work so that fraud, waste, and compliance issues do not slip by quietly. We help teams focus on the few situations that need a person, not every routine blip in the data.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable for the primary search: it relies on application- and industry-specific log formats, not a normalized CIM data model. Optional mapping to CIM, if you adopt it, is a separate follow-on project.
